title: Defeating Nondeterminism in Your Writing Workflow
text:
Today’s leading language models contain upwards of a trillion parameters, pretrained on tens of trillions of tokens. The transformer architecture that underpins them (Vaswani et al. 2017) keeps improving with scale, as these trillions are necessary for learning and representing all the patterns in written-down human knowledge.
In contrast, post-training involves smaller datasets and generally focuses on narrower domains of knowledge.1 This post is a placeholder that demonstrates the typography and layout of the blog, including citations, footnotes, and sidenotes.

title: Defeating Nondeterminism in Your Writing Workflow
text:
A second-level heading
Body copy is set in Iowan Old Style at 17px with a generous line height, matching the reference design. Links like this one pick up the subtle underline treatment, and inline code is rendered in red monospace.

An italic third-level heading
Lists use square bullets:

First point about something interesting
Second point with a bit more detail
Third point to round things out


Block quotes are styled with a thin left rule and a muted foreground color, exactly as on the reference site.

title: Defeating Nondeterminism in Your Writing Workflow
text:
Math and code
Inline math such as e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0 renders via KaTeX, and display math works too:

\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}}\big[ -\log p_\theta(x) \big]

A fenced code block, here distilling a teacher into a student (Hinton, Vinyals, and Dean 2015):
def distill(student, teacher, batch):
    logits_s = student(batch)
    logits_t = teacher(batch).detach()
    return kl_divergence(logits_s, logits_t)

Closing thoughts
Replace this file with your real writing. New posts dropped into posts/ show up on the blogroll automatically. Add references to references.bib and cite them with [@key]; footnotes ([^id]) and ::: {.column-margin} blocks both surface in the right margin.

title: Defeating Nondeterminism in Your Writing Workflow
text:
References


Hinton, Geoffrey, Oriol Vinyals, and Jeff Dean. 2015. “Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network.” arXiv Preprint arXiv:1503.02531.


Vaswani, Ashish, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Lukasz Kaiser, and Illia Polosukhin. 2017. “Attention Is All You Need.” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems.

title: Modular Manifolds: A Short Note

title: Modular Manifolds: A Short Note
text:
Why this matters
Constraining weight matrices to well-conditioned manifolds can make optimization better behaved. The details are left as an exercise; this paragraph is here to give the page some body text and demonstrate spacing between sections.
